However, the fact that a default profile reload replaced a custom template and that a perimeter patch altered templates/policies implies insufficient template/version control and inadequate pre/post‑change validation across firewall and reverse‑proxy/WAF configurations. [Data: Entities (612,629,628,631); Relationships (1088,1092,1087,1062)] REPORT 93: # Perimeter Firewall, Reverse‑Proxy/WAF & Voice NAT (Zurich/Basel) This community centers on perimeter and site firewalls, a reverse‑proxy/WAF, NAT rules for published services, and an externally‑accessible FTP footprint that was decommissioned. A weekend security patch and an emergency security‑hardening change caused template/profile reloads and an outbound restriction: the perimeter change activated a default inspection profile (Default‑Strict) on the reverse‑proxy/WAF which replaced a custom Portal‑App‑Template and altered header handling producing 502s; separately, an outbound restriction at the Zurich edge blocked RTP media to the SBC and produced no audio for external calls. Basel’s site showed a standard port configuration and correct port 443/NAT entries, pointing to NAT/state timeout interactions rather than simple port blocking as the cause of voice degradation. An employee‑recorded removal of a firewall policy on 17.12.24 resulted in decommissioning of externally‑accessible FTP. The corpus provides logs and change links that correlate these configuration changes with observed service failures. [Data: Entities (303, 310, 336, 304, 632); Relationships (497, 738, 502, 541, 1095)] ## Reverse‑proxy/WAF profile reload (Default‑Strict) replaced custom template and caused header handling regressions and 502s A security patch/reload resulted in the default inspection/profile "Default‑Strict" being activated on the reverse‑proxy/WAF, which replaced the previously used custom Portal‑App‑Template and altered header handling; this change is directly linked by ticket notes to 502 errors and header removal observed after the patch [Data: Entities (310); Relationships (497)]. The records show a template/profile-level change on the reverse‑proxy/WAF rather than an application‑level code change, indicating configuration drift at the perimeter layer is responsible for application‑facing HTTP errors; this makes template management and configuration rollback capability primary risk/mitigation points for restoring service quickly after future patches [Data: Entities (310); Relationships (497)]. ## Emergency outbound restriction at Zurich blocked RTP to SBC and caused no audio for external calls Investigation by the Network Team identified an emergency security‑hardening change at the Zurich edge that introduced a new outbound restriction blocking client RTP ports to the Session Border Controller (SBC), which produced no audio for users calling external parties [Data: Entities (303)]. Firewall logs corroborated the timing and presence of the outbound restriction rule, supporting a causal link between the firewall change and the onset of audio issues; the logs were pivotal in correlating change time to the incident start time [Data: Entities (444); Relationships (738)]. ## Basel site: standard port rules present; NAT and session/timeout behavior implicated rather than port blocking Checks at the Basel site validated the standard port configuration and confirmed that port 443 rules and NAT entries were present and correct, demonstrating that simple port closures were not the cause of service regression there [Data: Entities (336, 304); Relationships (541, 502)]. Because ports and NAT entries appeared correct, the analysis points to NAT/state timeout interactions (or asymmetric NAT behaviour) as contributing factors to voice impact, indicating a need to review NAT timeouts and session persistence for Voice VLAN/NAT policies to prevent intermittent media failures under patched or hardened firewall states [Data: Entities (303); Relationships (502, 541)]. ## Perimeter firewall patch produced configuration drift affecting reverse‑proxy/WAF behavior The perimeter firewall received a security patch/upgrade over the same weekend that—despite logs not showing obvious drops—overwrote or altered templates/policies and caused configuration drift that affected reverse‑proxy/WAF behavior; this is noted explicitly in the firewall analysis summary [Data: Entities (303)]. The combination of silent template/policy changes on perimeter firewalls and their downstream effects on WAF/reverse‑proxy headers creates a high‑impact failure mode because application layer failures (502s, header loss) can appear unrelated to the original network/security change without clear cross‑team change visibility [Data: Entities (303); Relationships (497)]. ## External FTP decommissioning was performed by an identified employee removing the governing firewall policy An externally‑accessible FTP service footprint was decommissioned on 17.12.24 when the firewall policy governing it was removed; ticket evidence records Employee 28 as the person who removed that firewall policy during decommissioning [Data: Entities (632, 630, 622); Relationships (1077, 1095, 1094)]. This shows a concrete linkage between a personnel action (policy removal) and service decommissioning; the records provide an auditable trail but also highlight that policy removals should be coordinated with service owners and change control to avoid accidental business impact where dependencies exist [Data: Entities (632, 630, 622); Relationships (1077, 1095)]. ## Firewall logs were useful for correlation but change tracking and template management are weak points Firewall logs revealed the outbound restriction rule and helped correlate the firewall change to incident timing, demonstrating that logging was effective for forensic correlation in this incident [Data: Entities (444); Relationships (738)]. However, the fact that a default profile reload replaced a custom template and that a perimeter patch altered templates/policies implies insufficient template/version control and inadequate pre/post‑change validation across firewall and reverse‑proxy/WAF configurations. Strengthening template management, change staging, and automated config drift detection would reduce the chance of template reversion or silent policy overwrites during patches [Data: Entities (310, 303); Relationships (497)]. ## Cross‑site differences (Zurich vs Basel) indicate inconsistent change impact and the need for standardized firewall/NAT voice handling Zurich experienced a clear outbound restriction blocking RTP, while Basel’s ports and NAT entries were validated as standard and correct; this divergence reflects inconsistent change effects across sites and the potential for site‑specific policies or stateful behaviours (NAT/timeouts) to produce different outcomes from the same patch window [Data: Entities (303, 336, 304); Relationships (541, 502)]. To mitigate similar multi‑site incidents, the organization should standardize voice NAT policies, document expected NAT state/timeout settings for Voice VLANs, and include voice‑specific acceptance tests in patch/change windows to quickly detect media disruption that may not surface in basic port checks [Data: Entities (303, 304); Relationships (502)]. ## Operational risk: weekend/emergency changes with inadequate rollback and coordination increase outage likelihood The incident chain shows emergency security hardening and a weekend perimeter patch coinciding with user‑facing outages (voice no‑audio and 502 errors) and an unintended service decommissioning, indicating that out‑of‑hours changes without robust coordination, rollback, and verification can produce high operational risk [Data: Entities (303, 310, 632); Relationships (497, 738, 1077)]. This supports instituting stricter change windows, mandatory pre‑change impact assessments (including downstream WAF and NAT interactions), retained pre‑patch configuration snapshots, and automated post‑change functional tests (HTTP header checks and RTP/SBC call tests) to ensure rapid detection and rollback when changes have unanticipated effects [Data: Entities (303, 310); Relationships (497)].

A weekend security patch and an emergency security‑hardening change caused template/profile reloads and an outbound restriction: the perimeter change activated a default inspection profile (Default‑Strict) on the reverse‑proxy/WAF which replaced a custom Portal‑App‑Template and altered header handling producing 502s; separately, an outbound restriction at the Zurich edge blocked RTP media to the SBC and produced no audio for external calls. Basel’s site showed a standard port configuration and correct port 443/NAT entries, pointing to NAT/state timeout interactions rather than simple port blocking as the cause of voice degradation. An employee‑recorded removal of a firewall policy on 17.12.24 resulted in decommissioning of externally‑accessible FTP. The corpus provides logs and change links that correlate these configuration changes with observed service failures. [Data: Entities (303, 310, 336, 304, 632); Relationships (497, 738, 502, 541, 1095)] ## Reverse‑proxy/WAF profile reload (Default‑Strict) replaced custom template and caused header handling regressions and 502s A security patch/reload resulted in the default inspection/profile "Default‑Strict" being activated on the reverse‑proxy/WAF, which replaced the previously used custom Portal‑App‑Template and altered header handling; this change is directly linked by ticket notes to 502 errors and header removal observed after the patch [Data: Entities (310); Relationships (497)]. The records show a template/profile-level change on the reverse‑proxy/WAF rather than an application‑level code change, indicating configuration drift at the perimeter layer is responsible for application‑facing HTTP errors; this makes template management and configuration rollback capability primary risk/mitigation points for restoring service quickly after future patches [Data: Entities (310); Relationships (497)]. ## Emergency outbound restriction at Zurich blocked RTP to SBC and caused no audio for external calls Investigation by the Network Team identified an emergency security‑hardening change at the Zurich edge that introduced a new outbound restriction blocking client RTP ports to the Session Border Controller (SBC), which produced no audio for users calling external parties [Data: Entities (303)]. Firewall logs corroborated the timing and presence of the outbound restriction rule, supporting a causal link between the firewall change and the onset of audio issues; the logs were pivotal in correlating change time to the incident start time [Data: Entities (444); Relationships (738)]. ## Basel site: standard port rules present; NAT and session/timeout behavior implicated rather than port blocking Checks at the Basel site validated the standard port configuration and confirmed that port 443 rules and NAT entries were present and correct, demonstrating that simple port closures were not the cause of service regression there [Data: Entities (336, 304); Relationships (541, 502)]. Because ports and NAT entries appeared correct, the analysis points to NAT/state timeout interactions (or asymmetric NAT behaviour) as contributing factors to voice impact, indicating a need to review NAT timeouts and session persistence for Voice VLAN/NAT policies to prevent intermittent media failures under patched or hardened firewall states [Data: Entities (303); Relationships (502, 541)]. ## Perimeter firewall patch produced configuration drift affecting reverse‑proxy/WAF behavior The perimeter firewall received a security patch/upgrade over the same weekend that—despite logs not showing obvious drops—overwrote or altered templates/policies and caused configuration drift that affected reverse‑proxy/WAF behavior; this is noted explicitly in the firewall analysis summary [Data: Entities (303)]. The combination of silent template/policy changes on perimeter firewalls and their downstream effects on WAF/reverse‑proxy headers creates a high‑impact failure mode because application layer failures (502s, header loss) can appear unrelated to the original network/security change without clear cross‑team change visibility [Data: Entities (303); Relationships (497)]. ## External FTP decommissioning was performed by an identified employee removing the governing firewall policy An externally‑accessible FTP service footprint was decommissioned on 17.12.24 when the firewall policy governing it was removed; ticket evidence records Employee 28 as the person who removed that firewall policy during decommissioning [Data: Entities (632, 630, 622); Relationships (1077, 1095, 1094)]. This shows a concrete linkage between a personnel action (policy removal) and service decommissioning; the records provide an auditable trail but also highlight that policy removals should be coordinated with service owners and change control to avoid accidental business impact where dependencies exist [Data: Entities (632, 630, 622); Relationships (1077, 1095)]. ## Firewall logs were useful for correlation but change tracking and template management are weak points Firewall logs revealed the outbound restriction rule and helped correlate the firewall change to incident timing, demonstrating that logging was effective for forensic correlation in this incident [Data: Entities (444); Relationships (738)]. However, the fact that a default profile reload replaced a custom template and that a perimeter patch altered templates/policies implies insufficient template/version control and inadequate pre/post‑change validation across firewall and reverse‑proxy/WAF configurations. Strengthening template management, change staging, and automated config drift detection would reduce the chance of template reversion or silent policy overwrites during patches [Data: Entities (310, 303); Relationships (497)]. ## Cross‑site differences (Zurich vs Basel) indicate inconsistent change impact and the need for standardized firewall/NAT voice handling Zurich experienced a clear outbound restriction blocking RTP, while Basel’s ports and NAT entries were validated as standard and correct; this divergence reflects inconsistent change effects across sites and the potential for site‑specific policies or stateful behaviours (NAT/timeouts) to produce different outcomes from the same patch window [Data: Entities (303, 336, 304); Relationships (541, 502)]. To mitigate similar multi‑site incidents, the organization should standardize voice NAT policies, document expected NAT state/timeout settings for Voice VLANs, and include voice‑specific acceptance tests in patch/change windows to quickly detect media disruption that may not surface in basic port checks [Data: Entities (303, 304); Relationships (502)]. ## Operational risk: weekend/emergency changes with inadequate rollback and coordination increase outage likelihood The incident chain shows emergency security hardening and a weekend perimeter patch coinciding with user‑facing outages (voice no‑audio and 502 errors) and an unintended service decommissioning, indicating that out‑of‑hours changes without robust coordination, rollback, and verification can produce high operational risk [Data: Entities (303, 310, 632); Relationships (497, 738, 1077)]. This supports instituting stricter change windows, mandatory pre‑change impact assessments (including downstream WAF and NAT interactions), retained pre‑patch configuration snapshots, and automated post‑change functional tests (HTTP header checks and RTP/SBC call tests) to ensure rapid detection and rollback when changes have unanticipated effects [Data: Entities (303, 310); Relationships (497)].

Exact artifacts to gather before escalation (minimum packet of evidence) - FileZilla Server log lines (timestamped) with the failing sessions (USER, PASV/PORT, 150/226/425/426 or TLS errors). - Server netstat/ss showing listeners (port 21 and passive-range). - Client-side WinSCP debug log for the failing attempt (with timestamps and client IP). - Perimeter firewall logs (allow/deny) and NAT/conntrack snapshot for the same timestamps. - PCAPs: perimeter interface and server NIC for the same reproduce window. Helpdesk: pull FileZilla logs (±15 min around reported failure), netstat, and get the user’s WinSCP log and client public IP. 3. Coordinate a 5–10 minute reproduce window with Network and Core. 4. Correlate: look for PASV 227 reply IP (internal vs public) and whether client SYNs to the PASV port are translated and forwarded to 10.10.10.10. Brief protocol note (general knowledge) - FTP (PASV) uses a control channel on 21 and separate ephemeral server ports for data; NAT/firewall must allow and/or rewrite the PASV reply and forward the passive port range. If the server advertises a private IP in PASV replies or the passive ports are not forwarded, external uploads will hang while internal clients succeed
